We are hiring for a Software Engineer specializing in AI Agent Development in Santa Clara, CA. This role requires five days onsite and is not a typical AI/ML engineer position. We seek senior software engineers with strong industrial, operational technology, or data-center infrastructure experience who have built AI agents in production.

Overview:
We are looking for an experienced contract software engineer to design and build AI agents that support device onboarding and continuous environment monitoring for data center infrastructure. The agents will be developed on Microsoft Azure AI Foundry and must be production-grade from the start. This is applied AI engineering work, not research. The contractor will deliver agents that operate reliably against real device data, integrate seamlessly with existing platform services, and are structured for extensibility and maintainability.

Required Qualifications:
- 8 or more years of professional software engineering experience with a proven record of delivering production systems.
- Deep expertise in software design and architecture, with substantial use of AI-assisted development tooling.
- Hands-on experience building AI agents or agentic workflows in production environments, including tool use, state management, and reliable execution.
- Practical experience with Microsoft Azure AI Foundry or a comparable enterprise AI platform for agent-based services.
- Strong Python development skills, with a focus on extensibility and long-term maintainability.
- Experience with industrial device protocols, specifically Modbus, BACnet, and OPC-UA.
- Familiarity with digital twin concepts or asset registry systems.

Preferred Qualifications:
- Experience with additional OT protocols such as MQTT/Sparkplug-B or DNP3.
- Background in data center infrastructure, facilities management systems, or DCIM platforms.
- Familiarity with automated device discovery and configuration extraction across diverse hardware environments.
- Experience designing agentic systems with human-in-the-loop approval workflows in safety-sensitive or operationally critical contexts.

If you are interested, please send your updated resume to kiran.s@fusionplusinc.com.

💡 “LLMs don’t fail because they’re weak… they fail because they don’t know YOUR data.”
I saw this firsthand while working on an enterprise GenAI solution.
The problem:
 👉 LLM responses were generic
 👉 Sometimes inaccurate (hallucinations)
 👉 Not aligned with business context
That’s when we shifted to a RAG-based architecture.
🔧 What I implemented:
 ✔️ LangChain orchestration
 ✔️ Vector search using FAISS + Pinecone
 ✔️ Custom embeddings for domain-specific data
 ✔️ Azure OpenAI integration
📊 The result:
 📈 35% improvement in response accuracy
 📉 Significant reduction in hallucinations
 ⚡ Faster, context-aware answers
💡 Key insight:
 It’s not about using a bigger model —
 it’s about giving the model the right context at the right time.
In my experience building enterprise AI systems,
 RAG has been a game-changer for scaling reliable GenAI applications
👋 I’m currently open to:
 ✔️ C2C / Contract roles
 ✔️ GenAI / AI Engineering opportunities

We’re Hiring a Sales Manager to Drive the Next Phase of Growth at Edgistify

About Us – Edgistify
Edgistify is redefining how India moves, stores, and manages products. As one of the country’s fastest-growing integrated supply chain companies, we combine robust warehousing, 3PL operations, last-mile excellence, and our proprietary AI-enabled WMS – EdgeOS to deliver unmatched operational efficiency.
We partner with some of India’s most loved brands across FMCG, D2C, Pharma, Consumer Durables, and E-commerce, running high-performing fulfillment engines that scale with their growth.

Role Overview
We are seeking a high-energy, consultative, and target-driven Sales Manager to fuel revenue growth for Edgistify. The role involves B2B sales for warehousing, fulfillment, and logistics solutions across multiple sectors.
This position combines business development, solution selling, client relationship management, and pipeline building, requiring a solid understanding of supply chain challenges and customer pain points.

If you enjoy consultative sales, love turning conversations into revenue, and thrive in high-performance environments—this role is built for you.

Key Responsibilities

1. Business Development & Market Expansion
Identify and target high-potential clients across FMCG, D2C, Pharma, Retail & E-commerce.
Conduct detailed market research to uncover trends, white spaces & new opportunities.
Lead intelligent outreach via LinkedIn, email, events & industry networks to build a strong pipeline.

2. Discovery & Solution Mapping
Drive structured discovery calls to diagnose client challenges & operational gaps.
Translate business needs into warehousing, logistics, and tech requirements.

3. Proposal Creation & High-Impact Pitching
Develop customized proposals showcasing ROI, efficiency, and value.
Present confidently to CXO-level stakeholders with data-backed storytelling.

4. Negotiation & Closure Ownership
Lead pricing discussions, SLA alignment & contract structuring.
Manage RFQ/RFP responses with cross-functional collaboration.
Ensure timely closures and maintain high conversion ratios.

5. Onboarding & Handover Coordination
Work with Implementation & KAM teams to ensure smooth post-closure transition.
Set clear expectations and ensure stakeholders are aligned from Day 1.

What We’re Looking For
MBA in Marketing with 2 to 5 years of B2B sales experience (preferably in supply chain, logistics, SaaS, or related domains).
Strong presentation, negotiation & solutioning skills.
Ability to work in fast-paced, high-growth environments.

Job Location - Head Office, Thane, MH

Interested candidates can share their applications to:
hr@edgistify.com

Decagon went from 0 to a $4.5B company in 3 years. They hit $1M ARR in 6 months — with 2 people. Every hire before product-market fit lowers your odds of finding it:

The two founders built the first version in three weeks. Customers were signing $100K–$200K contracts almost immediately.

Their routine was simple: call customers during the day, code at night.

They didn't hire employee #1 until $950K ARR.

Most founders I meet get this backwards. They think hiring more people is the key to growth. 

But every person you add before PMF just adds overhead.

More people means more coordination. More meetings. More process. 

The founders who find PMF fastest almost always look the same: tiny team, no layers. Their only focus is SPEED.

For most AI apps, 2-5 people is all you need. 

After that, each new hire actually lowers your odds.

Not because they're bad hires. But because you stop doing the only things that matter — talking to users and shipping fast.

Decagon didn't scale to 225 people and a $4.5B valuation because they hired aggressively early.

They scaled because early on, they didn't hire at all.

On March 30, Governor Newsom signed an executive order requiring AI companies that want California state contracts to certify their policies on bias prevention, civil rights, and privacy safeguards.

Four months earlier, President Trump signed an executive order directing the Attorney General to create a task force to challenge state AI laws. It conditions federal funding on states not enacting "onerous" AI regulations.

Two governments. Opposite directions. And every company operating across state lines is stuck in the middle.

The scope of what's already on the books is worth spelling out. California has over 20 AI laws taking effect in 2026 covering transparency, content safety, algorithmic pricing, and healthcare AI. Colorado's AI Act goes live June 30, requiring annual impact assessments for high-risk AI systems. Illinois bans AI-driven hiring discrimination and prohibits using ZIP codes as proxies for protected characteristics. New York City mandates annual bias audits for automated employment tools. Texas's responsible AI governance act took effect January 1.

None of these are harmonized with each other.

What I hear from every enterprise we talk to is the same thing: nobody is debating whether AI will be regulated. They're trying to figure out how to comply with five or six different frameworks at once, while the federal government is actively trying to invalidate some of them.

This is why I think governance is becoming an infrastructure question, not a compliance question. If your governance model requires manual reconfiguration every time a new state law takes effect, it breaks the moment the third or fourth regulation shows up. And we're well past that point.
The organizations I see getting ahead of this are building governance at the infrastructure layer. One system that enforces policy across every AI interaction, adaptable to whatever regulatory framework applies, without rebuilding compliance from scratch each time something changes.
That's how we built Trussed. One governance layer. Every AI interaction. Whatever the regulatory environment requires.

How many different AI governance standards apply to your organization today?

Every organization has a customer onboarding process. Almost none can show it to you.

The process lives in tribal knowledge, half-remembered training sessions, and a Confluence page last updated nineteen months ago. If the three people who know how it really works leave in the same quarter, the process leaves with them.

This is not a documentation failure. It is an architecture failure.

In software engineering, API-first means: define the contract before writing the implementation. The contract is the truth. Implementations are swappable.

Applied to business processes: define every process (hiring, onboarding, support, financial close) as a formal, machine-readable specification. Version-controlled. Forkable. AI-readable.

Toyota solved this for manufacturing with standardized work instructions and kaizen. API-first process management solves it for knowledge work with one critical upgrade: when your process spec is machine-readable, AI agents can execute steps, monitor compliance, and suggest improvements as pull requests.

What does this have to do with branding?

If brand identity is a function, the organization that runs it is a function composition. Product development, customer support, hiring, communication – each process generates brand signals. The brand's spectral signature is an emergent property of how these processes are configured.

When the processes are tacit, the brand is accidental. When the processes are formal specifications, the brand is deliberate.

Your brand is a config file. The question is whether you wrote it or it wrote itself.

- Quebec is doubling electricity rates for large facilities
- Result? Hyperscale AI projects are quietly routing around both provinces

Meanwhile in Alberta:
- 20+ GW of proposals flooding in
- Gigawatt-scale hyperscale builds actually moving forward
- On-site power + flexible policy = zero bottlenecks
- US hyperscalers choosing Calgary over Montreal

Make it make sense.

I spoke to US client last week who crossed BC and Quebec off their shortlist before the call even started.

Not because of talent.
Not because of fibre.
Because of policy.

And in IT hiring? We’re already seeing it:
- More contract roles tied to Alberta builds
- More US partnerships flowing west
- Fewer large-scale AI opportunities in BC and Quebec

My brother in tech…
BC and Quebec have the cleanest energy on the continent.
And they’re using it like a scarcity lever.

Alberta?
“Build first, figure it out later.”

And right now… it’s winning.
This isn’t an energy story.
It’s a policy story.

The Workday lawsuit just changed the rules.

Not just for Workday. For every business using AI to screen candidates.

Here's what actually happened:

A federal judge ruled that Workday wasn't just a software vendor. It was an "agent" actively participating in hiring decisions. The EEOC backed the claims. The case became a nationwide collective action for age discrimination.

The legal framing shifted overnight.

Before: "The algorithm decided, not us."
After: If your AI tool discriminates, you're exposed. Intent doesn't matter.

That's the part most HR teams are missing.

You don't have to build the bias into the tool. You just have to use it.

If you're running candidates through AI screening software right now, ask yourself:

→ Has it been independently audited for bias?
→  Does your contract clarify liability?
→ Do you even know how it filters people out?

Most teams can't answer those questions.

That's not a technology problem. That's a governance problem.

The Workday case won't be the last. More states are mandating annual bias audits. More plaintiffs are watching.

Are you confident in the AI tools your hiring process depends on?

Most companies building with AI don't know what regulatory tier their systems fall into.

That's not a minor gap. Under the EU AI Act, misclassification can mean fines of up to €35M or 7% of global annual revenue.

A study of 106 enterprise AI systems found that nearly 40% were misclassified by the teams that built them.

Here's the four-tier framework you need to know:

TIER 1 -- PROHIBITED (banned since February 2025)
These are outright illegal in the EU:
- Social scoring by governments
- Real-time biometric surveillance in public spaces (with narrow exceptions)
- Emotion recognition in workplaces and schools
- Manipulative AI that exploits psychological vulnerabilities

If your vendor is offering any of this, that contract is a liability.

TIER 2 -- HIGH RISK (compliance deadline: August 2026)
These systems require conformity assessments, technical documentation, human oversight, and ongoing monitoring:
- CV screening and candidate ranking tools
- Credit scoring and loan decisioning
- Medical devices and diagnostic AI
- AI in critical infrastructure
- Law enforcement and border control systems

This is where most misclassification happens. Teams label their hiring tool "decision support" and assume it's minimal risk. It's not. If it influences who gets shortlisted, it's high-risk.

TIER 3 -- LIMITED RISK
These systems have transparency obligations -- users must know they're interacting with AI:
- Chatbots and virtual assistants
- AI-generated content
- Deepfake detection systems

TIER 4 -- MINIMAL RISK
Spam filters, AI in video games, recommendation engines. No specific obligations, though best practices still apply.

The trap most teams fall into: they classify based on what they intend the tool to do, not how it's actually being used. A performance management tool used to inform layoff decisions is high-risk regardless of how it was sold to you.

The compliance window is closing. High-risk systems need to be conformant by August 2026. That's not far away when you factor in the 32-56 week realistic compliance timeline.

Where to start: audit your AI inventory against these four tiers. You'll likely find surprises.

I cover the full EU AI Act classification framework -- including a live decision-tree audit exercise -- in my AI Governance workshop on May 16.

1. Employment Contract & Compensation Policy
Salary hikes are governed by employment contracts + company HR policies
Unless contractually guaranteed, increments are discretionary, not a legal right

 Companies like TCS use “performance-linked incentives”, which legally allows flexibility in pay distribution.
2. Doctrine of Legitimate Expectation
Employees may expect annual hikes based on past practice
But legally, no enforceable claim exists unless explicitly promised
3. Labour Law Compliance (India)
Salary changes must comply with:
Industrial Disputes Act 1947
Shops and Establishments Act

 Employers can revise salaries, but cannot reduce wages arbitrarily without legal consequences.
4. Strategic Workforce Restructuring
Simultaneous salary hikes + reduced headcount reflects:
Legal workforce restructuring
Cost optimization without violating labour laws

Workday is facing a federal discrimination lawsuit over its AI hiring tools.

The case is Mobley v. Workday. It was filed in 2023. In July 2024, a federal judge refused to dismiss key claims, allowing the case to move forward on the theory that a vendor performing screening functions normally handled by employers may still face liability. 

Discovery is ongoing, and the class certification schedule now runs into 2026.  

The plaintiff alleges he applied to more than 100 jobs through employers using Workday and was rejected every time, often quickly. He claims the screening system disproportionately filtered out applicants based on race, age, and disability.  

That is what makes this case so important.

This is not being framed as a rogue employee problem. It is being framed as a Governance problem.

Here is what happens inside a lot of organizations:

* Procurement thinks IT vetted the vendor.
* IT thinks Legal reviewed the contract.
* Legal thinks the business defined the risk tolerance.

Nobody actually owns the decision.

And when that happens, the exposure does not stay with the tool.

The companies getting this right are not just buying AI tools. They are building governance frameworks before they sign anything. They’re assigning ownership, validating outcomes, and stress testing risk before automation touches a real decision. 

The Mobley ruling matters because it signals that handing work off to software does not automatically hand off accountability.  

The ones getting this wrong are one active federal case away from a question nobody wants to answer under oath:

“Who reviewed this before you signed it?”

The death of "global" remote work (Remote Global is falling)

1. The most striking trend: vacancies that allow working "from anywhere in the world" (Remote Global) are shrinking, while "local" remote work (only from the USA, only from the EU) is growing rapidly.
Python: Global remote work dropped by 15% (from 1,251 to 1,067 vacancies). Meanwhile, remote work in the US (only for residents/those located in the US) soared from 833 to 1,137.
Java: Global remote work decreased from 398 to 345. At the same time, demand for local remote work in Europe jumped from 162 to 242.
Conclusion: Companies are increasingly refusing to hire digital nomads from "anywhere." Employers want their remote employees to be in their tax/legal jurisdiction, or at least in their time zone.

2. The USA is the absolute locomotive of the AI market
If you want to work with artificial intelligence, you need to look for jobs in the American market.
In April, out of all vacancies somehow related to AI (Python AI, Java Spring AI, Kotlin AI), the US accounted for 683 vacancies.
For comparison: there are 396 in Europe, and only 163 in Russia.
The US market is absorbing AI specialists like a sponge, and it is in the US where the sharpest jump in Kotlin AI hiring was noted (from 6 to 24).

3. Europe is experiencing a Java Spring boom
While the Java market is stagnating in the US (the growth of Java Spring vacancies there was a modest 9: from 33 to 42), Europe is seeing a real explosion in enterprise development.
The number of Java Spring vacancies in the EU grew 3 times in a single month (from 38 to 117).
If you are a Java developer, the European corporate sector is currently the most promising direction for finding long-term contracts.

4. The Russian market: Python AI is doubling
The local Russian market also has its own clear growth leader.
The demand for Python AI in Russia more than doubled: from 69 to 159 vacancies in one month.
Meanwhile, classic Java and KMP in Russia are stagnating (the number of vacancies for pure Java changed from 162 to 163, and KMP is stuck at 6 vacancies).

Summary: The technological trend remains the same (Python+AI lead, KMP is stalling for now), but the geographical trend dictates new rules. To get a good job right now, it is more profitable to position yourself as a specialist located in a specific region (Europe for Java, the US for AI) than as a "global" freelancer.

AI is making it harder to get in the door out of school. 

“A paper last month by LSE professor Luis Garicano and co-authors extends the idea that jobs are bundles of tasks, to consider whether the different activities in a job are a tightly bound bundle or something more akin to an itemised list of discrete activities. In the context of software, a contractor or junior hire generally falls into the latter group: these jobs are weak bundles, with daily work consisting mainly of writing code to spec — tasks that could be given to someone else (or AI) without any disruption to the workflow or the quality of the final product. Here, AI breaks off a large chunk of the job and leaves a role with substantially diminished scope (or obviates the need for that hire or contract). 

But senior developers, or coders working outside the tech industry in roles where their programming skills are combined with domain-specific expertise, tend to have jobs comprising tightly enmeshed and cross-functional tasks”

This makes all the sense in the world. The more finely discrete your job is, the easier it is for AI to do. More junior jobs have more discrete set of activities vs a tight bundle that includes a multitude of activities that can’t be separated and AI can’t do/or as well.  After all AI is a probabilistic machine that works better under tightly defined rules. 

So the slow down in hiring at junior levels is real, at senior levels that is less the case due to the reasons above. 

Here is the thing, if less young people get hired in jobs that they feel they are qualified for the more social unrest we will have. 

Or let’s cut to the chase, if less kids who went to top schools can’t get a foot in the door that’s what leads to revolution. No one really cares if some kid from never heard of your school can’t get a job, but they do when it starts to impact the elite. 

Sure there will be a shift to more blue collar work, but that’s not a long term fix. Blue collar jobs on average that pay well destroy your body. More people destroyed their bodies in horrible jobs in the 1870’s. In fact according to Robert Gordon (macro economist/historian), 80% of the labor force then worked in tedious or dangerous jobs. Now it’s more like 20%. 

We’re kidding ourselves that there won’t be social unrest in the developed world that we haven’t seen since the 1930’s start to play out if that percentage ticks up to 40%. No one wants to go back to an 1870’s world, when life was much more nasty, brutish and short. 

Article in the comments. Some of these kids may be your relatives. And now your hands are softer and fingernails cleaner. I’m sure your kids would be chuffed to work in a mine.

The "Entry-Level" Paradox: What’s actually happening in Clinical Research (2025-2026)

I see the posts every day. Talented, driven individuals—some with years of experience, others fresh and excited—hitting a brick wall of rejections. There is a specific kind of burnout that comes from pouring your expertise into a custom application, only to have it disappear into the ATS 'black hole' or return as a generic automated rejection.

But if we want to navigate this market, we have to look behind the curtain. We aren't in a "hiring collapse"—we are in a Structural Reset. 

Here is what is actually happening behind the scenes as we head into 2026:

1. The "Big Pharma" Trim 📉 
Due to patent cliffs and shifting venture funding, many of the industry’s largest sponsors are undergoing massive restructuring. When thousands of roles are cut from administrative and overhead functions, the market becomes oversaturated with veteran talent. This creates a "trickle-down" effect where experienced pros are now competing for roles that used to be entry-level.

2. The "Lean" Model & AI 🤖 
The industry is pivoting. Hiring is moving away from traditional manual roles and toward "Digital Science." The current market is looking for candidates who can bridge the gap between clinical operations and AI/Data Analytics. The demand is for operational efficiency.

3. The Outsourcing Surge 🔄 
While many sponsors are cutting in-house staff, Contract Research Organizations (CROs) are holding steady. The work hasn't disappeared; it has moved. We are seeing a major shift where trials, particularly in Oncology and Rare Disease, are being funneled almost entirely through CRO partners.

4. The "Small Site" Opportunity 📍 
While the focus is often on the major global players, small, local research sites are often the most active hirers right now. These sites are frequently more willing to offer the hands-on training that the "big players" have stripped away in their quest for a leaner model.

The Takeaway: Don't take a 'no' personally when the entire system is being overhauled. Empathy is helping each other see the behind-the-scenes shifts so we can pivot from being 'job seekers' to 'market navigators'.

To my fellow job seekers: The 2026 market doesn't reward "applying more." It rewards "pivoting faster." Focus on the data, look at the CROs, and don't overlook the power of the smaller research sites.

Let’s talk about it—are you seeing these shifts in your own search? 👇

Experience- 8+ Years
Location-Remote

Mandatory Skill: Should have strong experience in recommendation systems and agentic AI
Job Description: Recommendation Engineer (Machine Learning)

We’re looking for a Recommendation Engineer to design, build, and optimize machine learning systems that personalize user experiences across content, products, or services. You’ll work end-to-end from data and feature engineering to model training, deployment, and experimentation—partnering closely with Product, Data, and Platform teams.
Key Responsibilities:
Design and develop recommendation models using collaborative filtering, content-based methods, and hybridapproaches (including ranking and retrieval architectures).
Build and maintain scalable pipelines for user behavior collection, feature engineering, and batch + near-real-time data processing (e.g., Spark, SQL, streaming).
Train, evaluate, and fine-tune models using modern ML frameworks; productionize training and inference workflows on cloud infrastructure (e.g., managed training, endpoints, CI/CD for ML).
Define success metrics (CTR, engagement, retention, conversion), run A/B tests, and iterate based on experiment outcomes and model monitoring.
Implement model observability: data drift, model drift, bias/fairness checks, and automated retraining/rollback strategies.
Collaborate with engineering teams to integrate recommendation services into APIs, apps, and data products with reliability, latency, and scalability constraints.
Document system design, trade-offs, and model behavior for technical and non-technical stakeholders.
 
Required Qualifications:
5+ years of experience building and shipping ML models in production, ideally personalization or recommender systems.
Strong foundations in machine learning, statistics, and optimization, with practical experience in ranking/recall problems.
Proficiency in Python and strong SQL skills; experience with distributed data processing (e.g., Spark).
Experience deploying models to production (batch or real-time), including model packaging, inference APIs, and monitoring.
Solid understanding of experimentation and causal measurement (A/B testing, guardrail metrics, statistical significance).
Ability to communicate clearly, write design docs, and work cross-functionally.
Preferred Qualifications (Nice to Have)
Experience with large-scale personalization (embeddings, ranking systems, multi-stage recommenders)
Familiarity with ML platforms (e.g., Amazon SageMaker – training, pipelines, deployment)
Experience with cloud (AWS/GCP) and MLOps tools (model tracking, CI/CD, monitoring)
Understanding of privacy, PII handling, and responsible AI
Languages: Python, SQL
Data: Spark, data lakes/warehouses, streaming pipelines
ML: Deep learning frameworks, embeddings, ranking models
MLOps: CI/CD, model registry, experiment tracking, monitoring
Cloud: Managed ML services
